text: Toy Story made history last year as the first computer generated full-length feature film.
The film earned over $192 million domestically more than 300 million internationally.
It will soon be released on home video.
Its technology success have revolutionized the business of animation and opened the door
to endless possibility between Hollywood and the Silicon Valley.
Joining me now, Academy Award winning director John Lasseter.
He led the innovative team from Pixar Animation that brought the film to life.
Also, Pixar's Chairman and CEO Steve Jobs.
He introduced computers to the ordinary user when he co-founded Apple Computers.
He currently has next software and I'm pleased to have both of them here to talk about
what's happening in the world of animation and this marvelous relationship between film
and computers producing animation.
Let me start with you.
As a director.
What's going on?
I mean, what does Pixar do?
That's so extraordinary.
I think what Pixar does is so...
Pixar does it so extraordinary is that we have taken a look at this new technology computer
animation and we don't look at it as a way to replace any of the creativity or any of the
real art of filmmaking.
We still... we look at it as... these are just a great new expensive pencils.
That's what it says.
It's artist using these computers as an artist that Disney uses a piece of paper and a pencil.
Because the focus of what we do is still where it's most important and that's where the story
and the characters.
I think Toy Story is a success, not because it's computer-generated.
It's a success because it has the characters of Buzz Lightyear and Woody in the storyline
that really has captivated audience.
But then the question is, what did animation, what could you do because of new technology and
how fast or and how less expensive than the whole way?

It reminds me a lot of when photography was invented, everybody thought that painting was going
to get replaced.
And that is something that really just won't happen.
What computer animation does is it has a whole new look.
But the key word is animation.
Animation by definition is making a motion picture frame by frame by frame by frame by frame.
And that doesn't change.
We labor over every single frame.
And so I worked on Toy Story for over four years.
The full production was two and a half years.

The difference is that we use a lot less people.
Well, that's my point.

about 1981 was that it was three dimensional.

That what you would never been able to do in hand drawn animation.

So one person can't do it.

And I met the leader of this group, Dr. Ed Catmill,

Nothing different about what came out of the head of the animator.
The process is very much the same in the development of the story.
Animation is very different than live action filmmaking, because in live action filmmaking,
director starts with a script.
And then blocks out the shot goes to the set and films it from all different angles.
Cover's the scene 10, 20 different times.
Close-ups, long shots, two shots, so on.
Takes all that footage goes into the editing room and makes the film there.
It pieces it together, juxtaposes things, edits the film.
It's always the post production after the fact.
In animation, the actual production of the footage is so time consuming.
For expensive, it's really the time is why it's expensive.
We can't really afford to do a scene 10 different ways.
We have to do it, just every frame has to count.
So what we do is in the story development, you pretty much edit your film in advance.
Now what I mean by that is you start with the script.
And then what we do is we do storyboards, sort of the visual script.
Storyboards are new.
You know, with storyboards, one of the things that Pixar is, it
we don't want to replace, we don't want to throw everything in the animation film making
process out the window and replace it with new technology.
We use the technology where it's going to make the film better or make the film different.
Storyboarding, there's nothing better than a piece of paper and a pencil and a 4x8 piece
You got no story, you got no film.
And you got no story.
Then you do a story reel.
You cut that onto film.
In our case, we use a computer there.
We use a nonlinear editing system.
That's very common in the industry now.
But it makes so that we can look at those still drawings with scratch dialogue, scratch music,
sit back, watch it on a big projection video.
And that film will tell us if it's working or not.
If it's working in our story reels, when we animate it and put the color to it, it's going
to work even better.
If it's not working in the story reels, the animation won't say that.
Lessons that let us bathe a test and iterate our film before we make it.
Totally different than live action filmmaking.
And we believe it's one of the reasons that the hit rate can be substantially different.
That process we're talking about really isn't much different than Disney did snow white with.
But that's one of the things is that Disney and Pixar we labor over the stories.
I mentioned I work four years on Toy Story.
The first year and a half was Story Development alone.
We work so hard because the most important thing to me in creation of Toy Story was to make a film
that of course kids will like because it's all about toys.
I mean, most kids look at it as a documentary.
The parents are going to like it.
The adults without kids would like it.
And then teenagers, I have a 16 year old son named Joey and he was one of my most important test
I, how old, 16.

Teenagers tend to not like a lot of things.

But when it works, it can be phenomenal.
Snow White is 60 years old.
Who do you know that hasn't seen Snow White?
I know people on most continents, and I don't think I know one person that hasn't seen Snow White.
So the ability for these things to live for 60 or even 100 years is amazing.
And that's very different than the technology world that I've come from.

10 to 15 years.

If I'm a great animator.
And if I've got a great story idea, why should I come to you?
Three, I think.
The three reasons?
One, Pixar is a place more anywhere else in the world that they can get creative satisfaction.
We try to give these artists a project that they can be proud of for the rest of their careers.
That's a big thing.
I know because I've worked in a lot of places where I was working on kind of garbage.
And it's hard to work.
But secondly, in working with these people, I try so hard.
Even if it's a smallest task, to give them a little bit of creative ownership to let them

I don't tell people how to do it.
And so that is a really important reason.
And the most important thing is that Pixar, we have a lot of fun.

You know, since Snow White was released in 1928, which was the first animated feature film ever,
60 years ago, every major studio has tried to break into this business.
And until last December, Disney was the only studio that had ever made a feature animated
film that was a blockbuster of $100 million or more in domestic box office.
And last December, Pixar became the second studio in history to do that.
So it's a very, very rarefied ability to make these products.
And I think Pixar is really able to do this quite well.
